retrieved_docs.title: Cerebral Contusion -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Edema is a rapid response that typically begins within minutes of an injury, peaks over several days, and resolves within 6 days. In contrast, hemorrhage initiates and expands within hours, particularly around perivascular areas, reaching maximum accumulation within just 24 hours. Notably, red blood cells retain their viability for up to 5 to 6 months after the injury. Polymorphonuclear leukocytes act quickly, initiating their response within hours of the injury and remaining visible for up to a month. Within just 2 hours, degenerated neurons, including red neurons, become apparent, signaling the immediate impact of injury. Macrophages and scavenger cells emerge within 24 hours, reach their peak between 7 and 14 days, and then gradually decline, illustrating the body's dynamic response to the healing process. Hemosiderin and siderophages can be detected as early as 5 days post injury and can persist for years as indicators of the body's healing trajectory. Hematoidin pigment appears 10 to 12 days after the injury and persists for years within hemorrhagic lesions. Angiogenesis, the formation of new blood vessels, begins 5 to 7 days post injury and reaches its peak by 3 weeks, illustrating the body's remarkable capacity for regeneration. The astrocytic reaction involves initial protoplasmic astroglial proliferation starting 4 to 5 days post injury, followed by fibrillary astroglial proliferation from 7 to 10 days, culminating in the formation of a glial scar that is crucial for restoring the blood-brain barrier. Additionally, fibroblasts appear within 1 week of the injury and can persist for months or even years, playing a vital role in collagen production and the development of fibrosis. This complex interplay of cellular responses highlights the body's remarkable ability to respond to injury and facilitate healing.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Management of Frostbite -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In most cases, postthaw phase treatment begins only after physical signs of tissue damage become evident. Wounds are staged and assessed, usually more than 24 to 48 hours after rewarming. This delay often results from the absence of early recognition protocols and the difficulty in identifying frostbite during its initial stages. [17] [18] This diagnostic challenge may lead to the further loss of tissue that lies in a transitional zone between reversible and irreversible damage. Contributing factors include reperfusion injury and persistent or progressive vascular compromise driven by ongoing inflammation. [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Traumatic Aortic Injuries -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who initially survive a traumatic aortic injury are transiently shielded from aortic rupture and catastrophic hemorrhage by the aortic adventitia and surrounding mediastinal structures, akin to nontraumatic dissection pathology. However, delayed adventitial rupture may occur during hospitalization, and vigilance is warranted. The time course of aortic rupture is biphasic but unpredictable. The aortic intima and media rupture immediately following a traumatic injury. However, it is typically disruption of the outermost adventitial layer that leads to rapid clinical decline. Studies have revealed that the time interval between the disruption of the intima-media and adventitial rupture can vary from seconds to years.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Shock -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying molecular mechanisms for spinal shock remain largely unknown. Animal models have been used to study the pathophysiology of spinal shock, which elucidates the natural progression of injury in SCIs to some degree. After initial hemorrhagic foci in the gray matter, there appears to be significant protein accumulation in the gray matter of the spinal cord. Edema then ensues and peaks 3 to 6 days after the initial injury and can be visualized on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for up to 2 weeks. The slow process of central cord necrosis and vacuolization subsequently occurs, continuing for about 2 months. The characteristically thin rim of white matter surrounding the central core of necrosis remains intact throughout this process. Often, patients with SCIs begin losing neurologic function above the level of the lesion several days after the initial insult, primarily due to spinal cord pathway rearrangement. Once this process abates, normal function above the injury returns, although the exact time needed for this process has not been defined and may last from weeks to months. If a patient survives the initial injury but remains immobile, the area fills with gliotic tissue. [13] The transection of the spinal cord results in spinal shock and transient extinction of reflexes below the level of injury. The main pathophysiological mechanisms include: [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Trauma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Defects in mitochondrial function and structure are significantly prominent in the pathogenesis of multiple myocardial injuries. [11] Multifocal subepithelial contusions of ventricles might be evident. Several experimental studies have investigated morphological changes in the myocardium following cardiac concussion. [12] The heart's gap junction profile is crucial for ion homeostasis, the development of arrhythmias, and contractile function. Changes in connexin 43 have been linked to both ischemic and nonischemic cardiac injuries. [13] Blunt trauma may cause myocardial infarction associated with time-sensitive histopathological changes. In the first few minutes following a myocardial infarction, the increased distance of the cross stripe in the infarcted zone is notable. [12] Within the first 30 minutes, swelling of the mitochondria is significant. Myocyte edema, glycogen loss, and decreased myoglobin stainability in the immunohistochemistry staining are predictable in the following 30 minutes. Hyalinised myocytes are visible for 2 to 3 hours following the myocardial infarct. However, the first agglutinated sarcoplasmic tubes are typically noted after 3 to 4 hours. Primary evidence of necrosis and extended necrosis zone is visible in the first 4 to 7 hours and after 18 hours postinfarction. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Assessment -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammation is initiated at the moment of tissue disruption (Days 1 to 6)

retrieved_docs.title: Ventral Cord Syndrome -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Acute spinal cord injury occurs in two phases. The initial injury phase results from the initial insult to the cord; be it by direct mechanical damage or by disruption of blood vessels, axons or cells membranes. This initial insult triggers a pathophysiological cascade that takes place within seconds. The secondary injury phase results from the on-going endogenous triggers that cause tissue destruction. The secondary phase can further subdivide into the immediate (less than 2 hrs), early acute (less than 48 hrs), and subacute (less than 2 weeks) phases. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammation is an essential aspect of the innate defense mechanism of the body against infectious or noninfectious etiologies. This mechanism is nonspecific and immediate. [1] The 5 fundamental signs of inflammation include heat, redness, swelling, pain, and loss of function. Increased blood flow leads to redness and heat, while swelling results from fluid accumulation. Pain arises from releasing stimulating chemicals, and loss of function reflects a combination of factors. These signs are evident in acute surface inflammation but may not all be observable in internal acute inflammation, particularly within internal organs. [2] Inflammation can be categorized into 3 types based on the duration of the response to the injurious cause—acute inflammation, which manifests immediately after injury and typically lasts for a few days; chronic inflammation, which can persist for months or even years if acute inflammation fails to resolve; and subacute inflammation, a transitional phase from acute to chronic lasting from 2 to 6 weeks. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Renal Tubular Necrosis -- Pathophysiology -- Later injury
retrieved_docs.content: The extension phase consists of 2 major events: Ongoing hypoxia following the ischemic event An inflammatory response

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Trauma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on projectile velocity and other characteristics, gunshot wounds can cause significant amounts of shearing, stretch, and blast injury to surrounding tissues. The associated tissue ischemia and full-thickness necrosis may evolve over time, contributing to either a missed diagnosis or delayed presentation, both of which can substantially increase morbidity and mortality. Stab wounds generally cause less injury to surrounding tissues but can difficult to visualize if minimal/focal in character. The increasing availability of advanced imaging for trajectory determination, especially for gunshot wounds, is important in the assessment, diagnosis, and operative planning for EI. This may not be true for stab wounds, mainly to the lesser extent of tissue injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Abrasion -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The medicolegal investigation may require a biopsy of the abrasion to conduct a histological examination. The histological examination investigates the stage of wound healing to provide an estimate of the time of injury. The wound healing process involves coordinated cellular changes, including bleeding and coagulation, inflammatory response, regeneration, and remodeling. The regeneration process further involves migration and proliferation, while remodeling involves extracellular matrix protein and collagen synthesis and the formation of new parenchymal and connective tissue. These processes are four time-dependent phases: Bleeding and coagulation, which initiate immediately Inflammation also begins promptly. Regeneration commences within days and lasts throughout the primary part of the acute healing phase. Remodeling, which begins weeks after the injury and can persist for longer than a year. [10] [12] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Blunt Cardiac Injury -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients who survive BCI have less severe injuries that range from structural injuries to electrical and conduction disturbances. [9] An intramural hematoma is one such structural injury that, for the most part, has a benign clinical course that resolves in 4 to 12 weeks. Intramural hematomas can cause premature ventricular contraction and transient bundle branch block. Another common structural injury is papillary muscle rupture, which can lead to valve regurgitation and may require repair. [9] Septal injuries can present with a murmur or arrhythmia on an echocardiogram. This injury begins with contusion followed by necrosis and then delayed rupture. Hence, early diagnosis is important as the injury may be treatable. [8] [9] The most common dysrhythmia in BCI is sinus tachycardia, premature atrial or ventricular contractions, and atrial fibrillation. [4] [9] Tachycardia in the patient with trauma should raise greater suspicion of ongoing bleeding than BCI. Once bleeding is ruled out, BCI becomes more probable in the differential diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Posttraumatic Hand Stiffness (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: When the soft tissues of the hand are damaged due to trauma, subsequent wound healing has 3 phases; inflammatory, proliferation, and maturation. [1] Firstly, the inflammatory phase is triggered by the disruption to the vasculature leading to exposure of the subintimal layer, with the movement of inflammatory leukocytes to the site of injury. Resultant edema leads to disruption of the gliding movement of the joints within the hand. In the long term, this protein-rich exudate will become scar tissue resulting in adhesions forming, causing further disruption to normal movement of the hand. [6] During the proliferative phase, there is a deposition of type III collagen within the joint capsule, ligaments, and tendons. In the final phase, there is scar maturation and contracture; this results in disruption to the hands' normal anatomy and subsequent joint stiffness. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hamstring Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic evaluation is an important aspect of the assessment of suspected hamstring injury to determine the nature, extent, and severity of the injury. Plain radiographs are useful to rule out a bony fracture, especially the apophyseal fracture. [16]  Ultrasound and MRI are the diagnostic modalities of choice. [17] Area of injury, as evidenced by edema and hemorrhage, is depicted by echotexture in ultrasound and high signal intensity in T2 weighted images in MRI. [18]  Ultrasound allows dynamic assessment of the injured hamstring muscles, but its sensitivity is increased if the ultrasound is done early after injury. MRI is still the investigation of choice to assess deeper muscle injuries, to evaluate a recurrent injury, and to differentiate new injury from the residual scarring. MR imaging also provides an estimate of the rehabilitation period. [19]  Though it can predict estimated time away from the sport, it cannot identify the risks for reinjury, which needs further study.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Inflammatory Response -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Acute inflammation initiates following a specific injury, triggering the release of soluble mediators such as cytokines, acute phase proteins, and chemokines. These substances promote the migration of neutrophils and macrophages to the inflammation site, representing a crucial component of the innate immune response during acute inflammation. [4] If the acute inflammation does not quickly resolve, it progresses to subacute inflammation. If inflammation persists beyond 6 weeks, it transitions from subacute to chronic, marked by the migration of T lymphocytes and plasma cells to the site of inflammation. Prolonged inflammation without recovery leads to tissue damage and fibrosis. [3] Additional varieties of cells, such as macrophages and monocytes, also contribute to acute and chronic inflammation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) serves as the anatomic locus for a wide spectrum of ocular disorders, encompassing inflammatory, traumatic, degenerative, metastatic, and congenital conditions. Disseminated choroiditis, as well as the combined entity disseminated choroiditis AND chorioretinitis involving the posterior pole, are inflammatory processes that manifest within the choroid. Similar inflammatory involvement is seen in focal choroiditis and in metastatic disseminated retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, indicating that both primary ocular inflammation and secondary metastatic spread can target the choroidal tissue. Scarring of the choroid and retina is also common; disseminated scar of retina AND choroid, post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar, and post‑traumatic choroid scar all represent reparative or fibrotic sequelae that localize to the choroidal layer after injury or inflammation. Degenerative changes are represented by partial generalized choroidal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 1144
retrieved_docs.content: The choroid (choroidea) is the anatomic site for a wide spectrum of ocular diseases and lesions. Inflammatory conditions that involve the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis, as well as nonspecific choroiditis, choroiditis‑retinitis, and focal choroiditis‑retinitis of unspecified etiology. Degenerative changes affecting the choroid are represented by age‑related choroidal atrophy, secondary choroidal degeneration, and the acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE). Traumatic and postoperative processes also manifest in the choroid, producing post‑inflammatory, post‑surgical, and post‑traumatic choroidal scars. Additional choroidal findings include serous choroidal detachment (including serous detachment of the eye), choroidal inversion, generalized choroidal calcification, and hereditary choroidal calcification. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s role as a common locus for inflammatory, infectious, degenerative, traumatic, and iatrogenic ocular pathology, highlighting the importance of recognizing choroidal involvement in the diagnosis, monitoring, and management of diverse eye diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: choroid scar all represent reparative or fibrotic sequelae that localize to the choroidal layer after injury or inflammation. Degenerative changes are represented by partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, senile choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), and secondary spread of choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), reflecting age‑related or progressive loss of choroidal structure and function. Traumatic disruptions of the choroid include choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي) and choroidal inversion (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while fluid‑related detachments are described as serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية) and general choroidal detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين). Congenital and hereditary abnormalities are captured by the terms congenital malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ

Perivascular deposits (بُقَعُ روث) are also recorded. Collectively, these entities illustrate the intersection of systemic vascular disease with specific retinal and choroidal hemorrhagic findings, neovascular disorders, inflammatory and infectious processes, traumatic and postoperative sequelae, and degenerative changes, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, therapeutic decision‑making, and prognostic assessment in ophthalmic care.

Scarring, a common morphological outcome of inflammation, trauma or surgery, includes post‑inflammatory choroid scar, post‑surgical choroid scar, post‑traumatic choroid scar, post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, disseminated scar

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 611
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical substrate for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies, including acute vascular events such as choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي) and choroidal tear (التمزق المشيمي). It is the primary site of numerous inflammatory disorders: disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, peripheral disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis, retinochoroiditis, unspecified disseminated retinochoroiditis (التهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد), choroiditis (التهاب المشيمية), combined choroiditis and retinitis (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية), and acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE, اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد). These inflammatory processes may involve the posterior pole or be widespread. Post‑injury and post‑procedure sequelae such as postinflammatory chorioretinal scar, postsurgical choroid scar, posttraumatic choroid scar, and retinal choroidal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) demonstrate healing‑related lesions within the choroid. Degenerative and calcific changes include secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), sclerotic degeneration (تنكس حصوي), generalized choroidal calcification around the globe (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in hemorrhagic, traumatic, inflammatory, postoperative, and degenerative eye diseases, guiding clinicians in diagnosis, monitoring, and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: (تنكس حصوي) and choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while localized calcification around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) represents a specific form of mineral deposition. Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

retrieved_docs.content: (e.g., retinal vasculitis, hypertensive retinopathy, retinal vein thrombosis, diabetic retinopathies), hemorrhagic and exudative events (e.g., subretinal hemorrhage, serous and hemorrhagic retinal detachments), degenerative changes (e.g., partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, drusen), and sequelae of injury or surgery (e.g., post‑inflammatory, postsurgical, post‑traumatic, postoperative choroid scars). Accurate identification of each sub‑condition under the umbrella of Disorders of choroid and retina is essential for precise diagnosis, appropriate coding, targeted therapeutic planning, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1080
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is a principal anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular diseases, serving as the locus for hemorrhagic events (نزيف مشيمي – choroidal hemorrhage), tears (التمزق المشيمي – choroidal tear), atrophic changes (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية – senile choroidal atrophy; انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية – secondary choroidal atrophy spread; تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة – choroidal atrophy; تنكس حصوي – calcific degeneration), serous and hemorrhagic detachments (انفصال المشيمية المصلية – serous choroidal detachment; انفصال مشيمية العين – choroidal detachment of the eye; انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي – hemorrhagic choroidal detachment), and sclerotic lesions (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي – partial choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي – total choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula). A wide array of inflammatory and infectious processes also involve the choroidea, including Disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, Focal choroiditis, Generalized disseminated choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Juxtapapillary focal choroiditis AND chorioretinitis, Peripheral focal retinitis AND retinochoroiditis, and unspecified widespread retino‑choroiditis (الالتهاب الشبكية والمشيمية المنتشر، غير محدد). Post‑inflammatory and traumatic sequelae frequently produce scarring (Postinflammatory chorioretinal scar, Postinflammatory choroid scar, Postsurgical choroid scar, Posttraumatic chorioretinal scar, Posttraumatic choroid scar, ندبة مشيمية شبكية – chorioretinal scar). The choroidea is also linked to بُقَعُ روث. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s central role in ocular disease assessment, diagnosis, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1112
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is the principal anatomic substrate for a broad spectrum of ocular disorders, encompassing hemorrhagic, inflammatory, dystrophic, fibrotic, degenerative, and calcific pathologies. Acute and massive choroidal hemorrhages (نزيف مشيمي, نزيف مشيمي باثق) represent direct hemorrhagic lesions of the choroid. Numerous inflammatory entities localize to this layer, including disseminated choroiditis, chorioretinitis of the posterior pole, peripheral disseminated choroiditis, peripheral focal retinitis, generalized or partial generalized choroidal dystrophies, choroiditis (التهاب المشيمية), combined choroiditis‑retinitis (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية), and focal unspecified choroid‑retinal inflammation (التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد). Post‑injury and post‑procedure sequelae also manifest as fibrotic remodeling within the choroid, such as post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scars, post‑inflammatory choroid scars, postsurgical choroid scars, and post‑traumatic choroid scars. Degenerative changes include secondary choroidal atrophy spread (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية) and serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية). Specific pigment‑epithelium disorders, exemplified by acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE, اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد), and familial choroidal atrophy (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), further illustrate the choroid’s involvement. Additional degenerative and calcific alterations described in the choroidea are تنكس حصوي (stone‑related degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (degeneration of the chorionic tissue), and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي (hereditary calcification of the chorionic structure). Collectively, these entities underscore the choroid’s central role in ocular disease, guiding diagnostic assessment and targeted therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 698
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is repeatedly identified as the primary anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies. Inflammatory disorders localized to the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis‑retinochoroiditis, as well as combined entities such as التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (choroiditis‑retinitis), and the unspecified focal variant التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد. Post‑inflammatory and post‑traumatic sequelae—post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar—are also situated in the choroidal tissue. Degenerative and structural changes affecting the choroidea comprise تنكس حصوي (calcific degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), and choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc in both partial (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and total (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) forms. Vascular complications recorded as choroidal findings include نزيف مشيمي باثق (persistent choroidal hemorrhage), hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), and non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين). The choroidea is additionally noted to have a relationship with بُقَعُ روث, although the clinical significance of this association is not detailed. Moreover, the choroidea is described in Arabic as “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating that pathological observations may be localized to a specific genetic chorionic region. Recognizing the choroid as the common locus for these inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, degenerative, vascular, and post‑injury scarring processes—and for its genetic and ancillary associations—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted imaging, and appropriate therapeutic planning in ophthalmic practice.

Partial and complete choroidal sclerosis surrounding the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي; حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) and hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect fibrotic stiffening. Focal choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة) and chorioretinal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) illustrate localized tissue loss and scarring that may involve both choroid and retina. The choroidea serves as the anatomic substrate for additional inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, postoperative and metastatic processes, including the entities listed above, metastatic disseminated retinitis and retinochoroiditis, partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, familial retinal dystrophy of the choroid, and choroidal tears. Massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي, نزيف مشيمي باثق) exemplifies the choroidea’s role as a site for hemorrhagic events. Collectively, these interconnected entities depict a comprehensive view of posterior‑segment ocular

Traumatic and post‑injury sequelae form another subgroup, with entities such as Posttraumatic chorioretinal scar, التمزق المشيمي (choroidal tear), and littken; choroidea (choroidal scar). Scarring and fibrotic changes are further represented by Disseminated scar of retina AND choroid, ندبة مشيمية شبكية (chorioretinal scar), and other choroidal scar lesions. Degenerative and calcific processes are also included: تنكس حصوي, تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي (global choroidal calcification), حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي (hereditary choroidal calcification), and انفصال المشيمية المصلية (serous choroidal detachment) as well as انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي (hemorrhagic choroidal detachment). Together, these conditions illustrate the complex interplay of inflammatory, vascular, traumatic, degenerative, and calcific mechanisms that can compromise retinal and choroidal integrity. Recognizing the

Traumatic and surgical sequelae are frequently observed on the choroid. Post‑traumatic choroidal and chorioretinal scars (Posttraumatic choroid scar, Posttraumatic chorioretinal scar) and postsurgical choroidal scarring (Postsurgical choroid scar, littken; choroïdoretinaal, postoperatief) represent fibrotic healing responses after injury or operative intervention. Additional scar‑related findings include disseminated retinal‑choroidal scar (Disseminated scar of retina AND choroid) and isolated choroidal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية). Degenerative changes are represented by partial generalized choroidal dystrophy (Partial generalized choroidal dystrophy), indicating a diffuse loss of choroidal tissue integrity. Peripheral focal retinitis and retinochoroiditis (Peripheral focal retinitis AND retinochoroiditis) illustrate localized inflammatory lesions

retrieved_docs.content: ocular, or hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين, انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Traumatic and postoperative sequelae are represented by choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي), choroidal tear, and a spectrum of post‑inflammatory, post‑surgical and post‑traumatic chorioretinal or choroidal scars (postinflammatory chorioretinal scar, postinflammatory choroid scar, postsurgical choroid scar, posttraumatic chorioretinal scar, posttraumatic choroid scar, ندبة مشيمية شبكية, choroidal‑retinal scar). Inflammatory and infectious processes encompass disseminated choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis and chorioretinitis, focal choroiditis, juxtapapillary focal choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral focal retinitis, retinochoroiditis, unspecified widespread retino‑choroiditis, unspecified focal choroid‑retinal inflammation, combined choroiditis and retinitis, focal combined
